data.title: The Rise And Fall Of China's Gold Premium
data.description:
By Cameron Liao

At a Glance

  • The premium of domestic gold prices in China over global prices has been on a roller-coaster ride, rising to record highs before tumbling into negative territory.
  • The volatility in the world's largest consumer of gold can be tracked to both demand and supply factors.

Globally, gold has been on a bull run since late 2022 amid strong central bank buying, a weakening dollar, rising geopolitical tensions and expectations for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates that materialized on Sept 18. At the same time, prices in China experienced even greater volatility, with the Shanghai gold premium - the difference between the domestic price in China and international prices - exhibiting extreme movements.

The premium at the start of 2023 was around $40 per troy ounce and reached an all-time high of $121.2 on Sept. 14, 2023. Just over one year later, the price is hovering around -$10/troy ounce, a whopping drop of over $130 from its peak. What happened?

China's Affection for Gold and Supply Constraints

The record Shanghai gold premium and the subsequent plunge was triggered by a combination of both demand and supply factors.

Gold has long been a beloved commodity in Chinese culture, and the country accounts for about one-third of global gold demand. As a symbol of wealth, store of value and investment tool, gold is widely held in many forms, including jewelry as well as coins and bars in China. The ongoing global gold price rally since late 2022 probably made the yellow metal even more attractive, especially compared to the lukewarm domestic stock markets and troubled property segments - both traditionally popular investment options. China's central bank has also been a significant buyer of gold, although it has paused purchases in recent months.

On the supply side, a short-term shortage was created by government intervention in the middle of 2023. The Chinese yuan had been depreciating since the beginning of the year amid a tightening of monetary policy by most major central banks and eventually reached a multi-year low in September. As safe haven gold is also utilized as a currency hedge, China's central bank decided to tighten gold import quotas to defend the weakening yuan.

The strong Chinese demand combined with the supply disruption facilitated a sharp upward movement in the Shanghai gold premium, which eventually set a record high. The import ban was lifted shortly thereafter and the premium subsided, but the Chinese gold market stayed elevated against international benchmarks into the first quarter of 2024, with the premium largely trading in the $20-$50 range.

Since the premium hit the record high, CME Group Shanghai Gold futures have gradually gained interest. Combined volume in both the yuan-denominated and dollar-denominated Shanghai Gold futures contracts average about one metric ton per day and account for roughly 35% and 65% of the trading volume, respectively. The two products can be used together to express views on the CNH exchange rate. The dollar-denominated contract is also often utilized together with the benchmark Gold futures to manage the spread between China and international gold prices.

The Mean Reversion of the Premium

As the first quarter of 2024 continued, the market soon started to show a slowdown in demand as jewelry consumption was impacted, likely by weak consumer confidence and rising gold prices. By the end of the second quarter of 2024, the price of gold at the Shanghai Gold Exchange was trading at around 550 Chinese yuan per gram, about 15% higher compared to the price at the height of the Shanghai gold premium in September 2023.

According to data from industry group the World Gold Council (WGC), China's consumer gold demand in the first three months of 2024 reached 309 tons, the strongest Q1 since 2014. It softened in Q2 to a total of 174.4 tons, registering a 9% year-on-year decrease.

A similar pattern can be observed in the monthly gold withdrawals from the Shanghai Gold Exchange (SGE). Meeting the demand for the Chinese New Year, January 2024 saw the largest ever load-out of 271 tons. Withdrawals, however, flattened and continued shrinking in the following months, with May, June and July figures all below 90 tons. The diminishing withdrawals coincide with the slowdown in local gold demand and are reflected in the lower Shanghai gold premium. By August, the premium had further retreated, fluctuating to around the historical mean of $6 per troy ounce.

Could Gold's Rally Continue?

It seems global gold markets are finding price support amid lower interest rates, continued geopolitical risks and potential central bank purchases. A survey of central banks by the WGC in June 2024 shows that 81% of the respondents expect the gold holdings of global central banks to continue growing in the next 12 months. On the other hand, profit-taking, market corrections and competition from other investment vehicles, such as the equity market, could mitigate the rally in gold prices.

If gold prices do stay strong, whether Chinese consumers manage to adapt to the high-price environment and resume purchases could be a factor dictating the premium price. Performance of China's economy, which has been slowing, and volatility in the yuan exchange rate could potentially play important roles as well.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ørsted: Seeing Some Stability And Upside From The Speculative 'Buy'
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) is a company that I have been reviewing for a few years now - and also invested in both before the last portion of the significant crash in 2022-2023, but also afterwards. I called it a speculative "Buy" in my last article on the company which you can find here - and the performance since that article has at the very least been stable.

This is a renewable player, with a focus on wind. It's also a player/company that has seen significant issues with profitability in many of its projects, which is fully reflected in the company's share price. However, there are certain changes and happenings that should be considered attractive and interesting here - and those, in conjunction with 2Q and a pre-3Q review is what I intend to give you and present you with here.

Despite some of the issues we've seen for Ørsted, the company is not an unattractive potential at the right price. I've considered going the options route a few times over the last few months, but given the impact this had last time around where I got stuck above a price I wanted to get stuck at, given the impacts of the earnings, I've been more careful with Ørsted as an options play.

At this point, we'll look at changing trends for Ørsted and where these could drive the company during 2024-2026E. When, if at any time, will the company turn around, and what would be the conceivable return if it did so?

Let's look at what we have here.

Ørsted and its performance - and future - in a changing and uncertain market

To say that Ørsted has issues, or has had issues, would be underestimating the seriousness of what I would consider a deeply troubling situation in the last 2 years. It's one of the reasons I have been on the fence and called Ørsted somewhat speculative in my last piece as well, found here.

One of the challenges faced by Ørsted is the poor returns for Offshore (and onshore) wind. It's what has thrown the company's calculations completely off, as these were based on entirely different interest rate scenarios and return rates for their projects. It is what drove the company to cancel several projects, simply because they were no longer profitable.

Since I reviewed the company last, we have 2Q24 results and are looking at soon getting 3Q24. The major piece of news that we have to look at is a shareholding item - namely that the Norwegian company Equinor about 2 weeks ago announced acquiring nearly a 10% stake in Ørsted at an MV of $2.5B. This transaction can be viewed in several ways, but the way that I view it is that Equinor, a very strong player in the field, has been calculating that regardless of the outcomes in the next few years, the price that's being asked for Ørsted shares on the market today, are deeply undervalued - and consequently, good use of capital is buying shares.

Why is this the case?

Conceivably, Ørsted is one of the utilities that is best positioned to take advantage of a decline in overall interest rates. This is because a reversal in these things would inch the company back to scenarios similar to the ones where the company's shares were significantly valued compared to today. A considerable catalyst of a decline in interest rates would be the farmdown of the Hornsea 3 project. This project has recently been resubmitted to the UK renewable auctions and has been effectively "derisked" as a result of this.

This transaction also turns Equinor, a company where I own shares, into the second-largest shareholder of Ørsted, with an ambition to increase its stake to 10% - but no higher than that. Equinor has also made it clear that it does not seek board representation.

The strategic rationale for this move is a good one. Equinor's current renewable portfolio is somewhat choppy and, in my view, lacks a strategic direction and underlying logic for its goals and targets. With the addition of Ørsted, this goes from being a merely symbolic and somewhat greenwashed move to being an actual solid part of the company's goals and directions.

So with that in mind, I view this as being a positive. This does not change my evaluation for Equinor, but it changes my valuation and estimation for Ørsted. It is now, to my mind, more attractive. The move that Equinor did is the less common move, but it implies that Equinor believes natively investing In Ørsted instead of developing more renewable assets is the cheaper option; ergo, the company believes Ørsted to be undervalued.

I would also like to speculate in this article that Equinor's investment means that they believe they do not see Ørsted going to the markets to dilute their equity with a rights issuance. If they believed this to be a risk, it goes to reason that they would wait and see after such a development.

Ørsted, as of the end of 2Q24A, has around 9 GW of gross offshore wind capacity, and 5 GW of net capacity, set to grow for the full year of 2024. The company is "the" leader in offshore wind, and 75% of the company's EBITDA comes from this. Previously, this sector has been under targeting from subsidies, and this enabled Ørsted to be a leader in this sector early on.

The issue, to recap here, was a sharp rise in construction costs and interest rates since 2021, resulting in a situation where none of the projects met the group's returns threshold of 150-300 basis points over the weighted average cost of capital. This resulted in turn in sanctioning only a very small number of projects, and removing over 2 GW of gross capacity in November of last year, resulting in 36BDKK impairments and cancellation fees.

This was the reason that the company suspended dividends, and increased divestment targets as well.

I will say again that Ørsted is a very good example of overreaction not being a good investment strategy. Even I was prone to overreacting here, and in retrospect, I should have spent more time on my calculations rather than focusing on the fallout and media circus that arose after the news about a year ago.

2Q24 operational performance was in fact good. The company's EBITDA was up 59%, with offshore sites up 40%. Full-year EBITDA is expected at 23-26B DKK, with lower bioenergy earnings but otherwise in line. The company has started its commissioning of capacity again. It's 2 GW of renewables in Taiwan and the US, and the commissioning of the Eleven Mile project, and the solar part of the Helena Energy Center. The company also showcased progress in Hornsea 3, the battery system, and saw the reversal of some cancellation fees, due to better-than-expected settlement outcomes. These are all positives, and worth noting.

Company debt coverage is up - 23% at this time, with interest-rate bearing debt in total down, so it's an uptick due to higher FFO - but the company's FFO coverage is expected to tick up over 30% by 2026E.

As we see more competition in the offshore sector, the company has previously upped its guidance and ambitions, only to lower them this year to instead focus on balance sheet fortification. This is likely to be ongoing for at least 2 years.

A number of positives to consider for 3Q24 exist. First off, the results from the recent US auctions are entirely positive. This also implies good pricing for future auctions. It also mirrors the 2024 development in the UK, with the extension of capital allowances. This capital allowance will, among other things, support value in Hornsea 3.

However, I won't move forward without mentioning some of the risks that still exist in the company. This includes, but is limited to things like higher interest rates, which are one of the primary share price impacts that exist here. A delay in some projects in Europe is also likely, exemplified by the potential delay in Dogger Bank A - this is not due to returns, but due to supply chain issues. The biggest risk is the business plan presented 9 months ago, which is entirely dependent on the divestment of assets, including the farm-down of Hornsea 3. If the company does not do this, the company would likely have to do a rights issue.

Let's look at the company valuation.

Ørsted is attractive here, but it's currently very speculative indeed

Ørsted's valuation is currently very distorted. I view the company's current P/E at almost 140x P/E normalized, but this is due to a negative 2023A result, that's expected to recover next year and the years after this. My current forecasts are for the company are for a normalized earnings level for 2024-2026E of around 25 DKK/share - lower than before, but still a decent level that implies a 17.2x P/E valuation based on the current price for the company today. That is a significant discount to the 5-year average of 28x, and even to the long-term average of 23x P/E.

It is here that I see the upside for Ørsted, and why I remain positive in my stance on the company - albeit a speculative one.

Using a 12x EBITDA multiple and around 23-24x P/E is the more conservative way to go here. Given current industry trends, I forecast no more than a 5.5-6% CAGR for the company's EBITDA, but excluding eventual farm-downs and cancellation fees, which are uncertain. Key drivers include new projects, and ramp-up of projects like GC1, South Fork wind, and Gode wind farms. But all of these things are to be impacted by things like wholesale power price reductions (which I have covered extensively previously in articles on utilities), and subsidy reductions - the entire reason why Equinor invested in Ørsted's instead of buying assets or doing their own - it's simply not as profitable anymore.

In the end, I do believe that Ørsted's current share price represents a significant discount to what the company should be worth, and what it might be worth in the future.

It is for that reason I consider the company to be a "Buy", and give the company the following target based on the following sort of thesis.

Thesis

  • Ørsted is a now-speculative renewables company that remains one of the relevant market leaders in the entire segment. It is, in fact, a market leader in Wind. It's also state-owned, has a well-filled pipeline, and is one of the most conservative financing pipelines and maturities when it comes to utilities.
  • However, a meager yield and premiums make valuation important - and I would only buy the company as long as it stays within its range. At current numbers, I would not buy it above 500 DKK per share and would put my PT closer to 440 DKK. The cut dividend and the changed outlook justify my cut in valuation, but also why I still believe the company is attractive here on a speculative basis.
  • That means that as of right now, Ørsted is a "BUY" for me, but it's a speculative one, and the upside is highly dependent on how the market for the offshore market develops, and how the 2024-2026E period works out. As of now, and expecting for 3Q24, I see a continued upside here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of 440 DKK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2024.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Demand Destruction For Existing Homes: Sales In 2024 To Plunge Below 4 Million, Lowest Since 1995, As Supply Spikes
data.description:
Because prices are way too high. Buyers’ strike deepened even with mortgage rates at 2-year lows at the time of those sales. But rates have spiked since then.

Demand for existing homes is wilting further, despite surging inventories and spiking supply and much lower mortgage rates - they hit a two-year low at the time these sales were made.

Sales of existing single-family houses, condos, and co-ops that closed in September dropped to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.84 million, the lowest rate of sales since the worst three months of the Housing Bust, down by 3.5% from the crushed levels a year ago, down by 38% from September 2021, and down by 29% from September 2019, according to data from the National Association of Realtors (NAR) on Wednesday.

But the impact of the recent spike in mortgage rates is still to come. These were sales that closed in September but were made in prior weeks and months when mortgage rates were much lower. Mortgage rates bottomed out in mid-September, then slowly ticked up after the Fed’s monster rate cut. They didn’t start spiking until the beginning of October, which will show up as a further hit to the closed sales data in a month or two (historical data via YCharts):

Too-high prices cause demand destruction on an epic scale

Actual home sales in September (not seasonally adjusted, not annual rate) dropped to 331,000 homes. For the first 9 months, actual sales fell by 2.8% from the already crushed levels of the first 9 months last year.

So we updated our estimate for whole-year sales in 2024, using the 9-month year-over-year decline as factor to estimate the remaining three months. Today’s sales figures push our estimate below the 4-million mark, to 3.97 million sales, the lowest since 1995.

This demand destruction, caused by the gigantic spike in prices - they’re now way too high - is even larger than during the Housing Bust.

But during the Housing Bust, demand destruction was caused by an economic and financial crisis as millions of people lost their jobs and mortgages blew up after years of reckless mortgage lending.

Too-high prices destroy demand, which is a fundamental economic principle that even home sellers cannot escape (light blue column = our estimate for 2024, historical data from YCharts).

Inventory and Supply spike to multi-year highs

Unsold inventory jumped to 1.39 million homes in September, the highest in four years, and up by 23% from a year ago, according to NAR data.

Importantly, inventory jumped in September, when it normally falls in September. Inventory normally peaks in June and then declines every month for the rest of the year, with the low point in December.

But this year, every month since June, inventory has risen instead of falling. Those are the vacant homes coming on the market even as demand has wilted.

Supply, given the wilting demand, jumped to 4.3 months at the current rate of sales, up by 27% from a year ago and the highest for any September since 2018 (4.4 months), and higher than in the Septembers of 2019 and 2017 (historical data via YCharts):

What the much lower mortgage rates since November 2023 have done is bring out the sellers, and so, inventory and supply have surged. But buyers have retrenched in their buyers’ strike because prices are way too high.

New listings jumped in September, when they normally decline in September, according to data from Realtor.com. These are the vacant homes that are now coming on the market that homeowners had moved out of, often years ago, but didn’t put on the market because they wanted to ride up the price spike all the way; and they already bought a new home back then, and so by not putting their homes on the market when they bought one, they contributed to the inventory shortage at the time. Now they’re trying to unload them, just as demand has collapsed.

Nearly anything will sell if the price is low enough. People in commercial real estate have figured this out, selling office towers 60% and 70% below their prior transaction prices.

Surging mortgage rates in October will weigh on sales

Mortgage rates have surged recently, but during the time these sales were made, they’d hit two-year lows. The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate started plunging in November 2023 from the 7.8% range and reached 6.14% at the end of September, the lowest since September 2022, according to data from the Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA). Then, in October, it spiked to hit 6.52% a week ago, and remained there, according to the MBA on Wednesday.

This weekly average hasn’t yet captured the moves over the past few days. But the daily measure of the average 30-year fixed mortgage rate by Mortgage News Daily jumped to 6.92% today. The impact of this jump in mortgage rates in October will show up in closed sales over the rest of the year.

The weekly average 30-year fixed rate via the MBA:

NAR blames everything except the problem: too-high prices

The median price by the National Association of Realtors of existing homes has exploded by 50% since the beginning of the pandemic in early 2020, most of it in the first two and a half years, fueled by the Fed’s interest rate repression and free money. It was the craziest 2.5-year frenzy ever in the housing market, and those now way-too-high prices have destroyed demand. Buyers are on strike, and they’re staying on strike.

But here comes the National Association of Realtors, trying to blame something other than the way-too-high prices: Last year, it had blamed the inventory for the collapse in demand, and then it blamed the surge in mortgage rates for the collapse in demand. But this year, inventories surged, and mortgage rates dropped. So both excuses are out the window.

So now it is blaming, you guessed it, the election:

“Perhaps, some consumers are hesitating about moving forward with a major expenditure like purchasing a home before the upcoming election,” the NAR said today.

The NAR has been blaming everything it can drag by its hairs except the actual problem: prices are too high and have killed demand. Much lower prices would stimulate demand - and commissions for realtors because they’d make more sales. Why is this so hard to come to grips with?

But instead, the NAR has started the long wait for wages to catch up with a 50% spike in home prices: “With wage growth now outpacing home price appreciation, housing affordability will improve,” it said.

Yes, but it will take a long while - years - before wages have risen enough to catch up with the 50% spike of home prices from already precariously high levels before the pandemic.

Commercial real estate has figured this out a while ago: massive revaluation has caused sales to pick up. And the homebuilders have figured this out too: they’re offering homes at lower prices and with big incentives and costly mortgage rate buydowns to sell the homes they built, and they’re running circles around sellers of existing homes.

The median price of single-family houses fell to $409,000 in September, the third month of seasonal declines from the seasonal peak in June. Year-over-year, the price was up by 2.9%, down from year-over-year price increases of 3.9% in July, 4.1% in June, 5.2% in May, and 5.4% in April.

The median price of condos and co-ops fell to $361,600 in September, which whittled down the year-over-year gain to 2.2%, from year-over-year gains in the +4% to +9% range late last year and earlier this year.

Unlike single-family house prices, condo prices didn’t book any year-over-year declines in mid-2023.

Home prices by metro vary widely

In some metros, prices have plunged, such as in the Austin metro (-20% from the peak), while in other metros, prices have risen to new highs, such as in the New York City metro. But in all metros they spiked insanely in the 2020-2022 period, which is now causing the demand destruction.

We discuss by-metro September prices here: The Most Splendid Housing Bubbles in America: September Update: Prices Drop in 26 of 28 Big Metros, even San Diego, Los Angeles, and these are two of the 28 charts:

Demand destruction has occurred in all regions

The charts below show the seasonally adjusted annual rate of sales, released by the NAR today, in the four Census Regions of the US. A map of the four regions is in the comments below the article.

Northeastern US: Sales fell to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 460,000 homes, the second lowest since the 1990s, just a hair above July 2010.

Midwestern US: Sales fell to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 900,000 homes, the lowest since the Housing Bust.

Southern US: Sales fell to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,720,000 homes, the lowest since the Housing Bust.

Western US: Sales ticked up to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 760,000, a little higher than some months in 2023 and 2024, but beyond that, the lowest since the 1990s:

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nextracker: Market Leadership And Growth Prospects Make It A Buy
data.description:
Nextracker (NASDAQ:NXT) is one of the greatest long-term solar investments I have found. Although it has had a tough year from a momentum standpoint, I believe the next 12 months will be much brighter. I estimate a 25% return over the period, with long-term growth likely from the continued expansion of renewable energy trends. With a market-leading position and a reasonable valuation at present, I assign a confident long-term Buy rating to the stock.

Operational Analysis

Nextracker is the leader in intelligent solar tracker and software solutions, with a focus on optimizing solar energy production and enhancing operational efficiency. Its core product offerings are NX Horizon™ Trackers (designed for optimizing utility-scale solar projects), its TrueCapture™ System (for yield optimization using advanced sensors, weather forecasting, and machine learning), and its NX Navigator Platform (designed for smart monitoring and control).

The company is involved in large-scale projects like the Sudair Solar PV Plant in Saudi Arabia. This highlights its role in international renewable energy expansion, and management is also expanding the company's manufacturing facilities, particularly in the U.S., to support higher production to meet the global demand for solar tracking solutions.

With over 100 GW of smart solar trackers installed worldwide, Nextracker has a proven track record of delivering reliable and efficient solar solutions. Moreover, with the lead in technology and market share, I expect the company will have a robust future ahead of it, and investing in it now should reward shareholders quite substantially (the valuation now seems appealing, which I will analyze below).

As solar projects become vaster and more sophisticated, it is undeniable that most serious users and utility-scale projects will seek efficient tracking and optimization solutions. Therefore, I think clever solar investors will view Nextracker as a long-term holding and monitor its valuation over time to ensure it remains reasonable.

Array (ARRY) is a company that deserves a lot of attention in this analysis because it is the second-largest solar tracker company in the world, behind Nextracker. I have covered Array before, assigning a Hold rating and outlining its short-term downside (since then, it has lost 50% in price). That said, Nextracker has experienced similar negative momentum recently. While this looks disconcerting, I think both companies are attractively valued now, and with 2025 set to be a robust year for the solar markets (primarily due to lower interest rates), both Array and Nextracker are set to benefit.

Array's flagship products include the DuraTrack® HZ v3 (including a patented design for one motor to drive multiple rows of solar panels) and the SmarTrack™ Software (a platform using machine learning and proprietary algorithms to optimize solar panel positioning). Both companies are positioned for long-term growth, so the real question is which of the two companies offers the best valuation and the highest return prospects.

Financial & Valuation Analysis

Both Nextracker and Array are similarly valued based on revenues, but Nextracker has a much stronger EBITDA, bringing its valuation down substantially on this metric's price multiple.

When looking directly at Nextracker, FY26 (which comprises the majority of calendar 2025) is likely to be robust for the company. It is experiencing high demand for its solutions, given that it is the market leader and the global push toward renewable energy continues. In Q1 2025, the company achieved a 50% year-over-year increase in its revenue, showing healthy demand in both the U.S. and international markets. Moreover, the company's order backlog reached $4 billion in the quarter.

Now, Q2 results are approaching on October 30. The company has recently expanded its manufacturing footprint with over 20 facilities, including new sites in Pittsburgh and Nevada; this raises its production capacity to over 30GW annually, giving it the capability to meet the high demand on the horizon in calendar 2025. In Q2 Fiscal 2025, this should help the company to maintain strong revenues. Moreover, the company's U.S. domestic content aligns with government incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act, providing tax credits to enhance the company's profitability. These are two core catalysts that will be accretive to the company both in Q2 and in the long term.

Based on the above growth catalysts and the fact that interest rates have already dropped somewhat, I think we'll see a normalized EPS of $0.90 and total revenue of $675 million in Q2—the company has consistently beat the consensus estimates recently. However, while management has guided for revenue of between $2.8 billion and $2.9 billion in FY25 (representing growth from FY24's revenue of $2.5 billion), the guidance for GAAP net income is between $369 million and $399 million for FY25 (this is lower than the GAAP net income of $496 million reported for FY24). The company expects a higher proportion of international revenues in FY25, which typically have different margin profiles to U.S. revenues, likely impacting overall profitability. Moreover, recent acquisitions of Ojjo and Solar Pile could have integration costs that put pressure on Nextracker's margins.

Based on my above analysis, I expect the company will achieve year-over-year normalized EPS growth of 6.5% in the 12 months following Q2 results. Based on my forecast, the company will have a TTM normalized EPS of $3.75 in Q2 2025 and a TTM normalized EPS of $3.99 in 12 months following this.

Given that the company's P/E non-GAAP ratio is currently 9.3 (55.4% lower than the sector median) and lower than Array's P/E non-GAAP ratio of 9.9, I don't think its valuation ratios contracting is likely for Nextracker over the next year. Instead, I would not be surprised if the P/E non-GAAP ratio has risen to 10 or above in anticipation of the robust growth likely in FY26 and beyond. To be conservative, at a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 10 and a normalized TTM EPS of $3.99, the stock will be worth $39.90. This is a 24.5% increase from the current stock price of $32.05. Therefore, I assign a Buy rating to Nextracker.

Macroeconomic Risk Analysis

I think it's worth taking a broad macro view of the solar industry to understand how tenable a long-term allocation to Nextracker is. There is a great paper in Nature Communications called 'The momentum of the solar energy transition.' In the article, the following chart is provided, and I think it presents a very compelling support to the Nextracker investment case. I recommend that readers follow the link to the paper and read its full insights. For those who prefer words to charts, the future of energy appears to be heavily reliant on solar.

In my opinion, now is the greatest time in history to invest in the solar markets. I believe it is a time to invest substantially in leading, well-managed solar companies and hold these stocks for decades. That said, there could be periods of resistance to the trends in solar energy adoption, including policy changes. Moreover, I believe a major world war would significantly reduce the likelihood of an uninterrupted growth horizon for solar energy. Therefore, investors will want to ensure that they support government leadership that continues to prioritize solar energy's proliferation and also invest in leadership that does not prioritize cyclical military revenues over green energy, technology, and other non-toxic forms of capital growth. From a macro perspective (which is my predominant perspective of analysis), the world is quite torn between industries that are significantly destructive (such as the military-industrial complex and fossil fuels) and a new emerging economy that could be less driven by these forces. Therefore, this tension and the interests of differing and conflicting parties could reduce the growth capabilities of Nextracker if the broader renewable energy thesis comes under inhibition from other competing capital and policy interests.

Conclusion

Based on my analysis, now is a great time to buy Nextracker shares. I consider the company a strong long-term investment, and I estimate a 25% 12-month return from the present price, given its valuation and broader growth catalysts. Moreover, I do not expect the macro trends in solar adoption to diminish, and I do see a future where the world is powered predominantly by solar energy—we will need sophisticated solar tracker systems to support this. Although I am also supportive of continuing fossil fuel use while we make a stable and resource-respectful transition to renewable energy, Nextracker is one of the companies I expect to prevail in the long run.

data.title: Apple: Sluggish Growth Numbers Can Change The Narrative
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) (NEOE:AAPL:CA) has made some big announcements this year, but the stock has performed modestly compared to the broader S&P 500 index. For YTD, Apple’s 22% total returns are slightly below the 24% total returns of S&P 500. Some analysts have noted that the demand for iPhone 16 is sluggish despite the hype around Apple Intelligence. Apple's upcoming earnings date is on 31st October. If the earnings data shows a YoY decline in iPhone revenue, we could see a big bearish sentiment towards the stock. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Wall Street is getting more cautious with the company's AI hype.

Apple is already facing headwinds against its Vision Pro device which is competing against Meta’s (META) lower-priced products. Meta has reported annual losses of close to $15 billion in its Reality Labs segment for the last few years and Apple would need to invest similar resources if it wants to gain a reasonable market share in this segment. Apple is also facing massive fines from EU and is already lobbying in order to get them reduced.

Apple’s valuation multiple is one of the highest among the big tech peers. On the other hand, Apple’s forward revenue and EPS growth projection are quite modest. If the earnings numbers are below expectations, we could see a major correction in the stock. Apple’s stock looks too pricey at the current valuation compared to other options available.

Sluggish demand can cause correction

Apple had hyped its new Apple Intelligence iPhone for some time. However, all the external data shows that the demand has been sluggish. According to TF Securities analyst Ming-chi Kuo the Y/Y pre-orders for pro models has been negative. A lot of bullish sentiment towards Apple stock relies on successful demand trajectory within the Intelligence iPhone lineup. If the YoY iPhone revenue declines in the upcoming quarter, it can cause a significant bearishness towards the stock. A YoY iPhone revenue decline of more than 3-4% can cause a big change in the narrative and may cause a strong correction.

Figure: Apple’s iPhone revenue in the last few quarters. Source: Apple Filings

Apple’s iPhone segment has already reported YoY 1% decline in the last quarter and for the first three quarters of the latest fiscal year. Apple still relies on iPhone segment for close to 50% of its total net sales. Apple’s iPhone forms the backbone for all other products and services within the ecosystem. A decline in this segment, despite the hype of AI services, could be a big headwind for the company.

Apple is already facing currency headwinds as mentioned by the management. Higher competition in China which contributes close to 18% of the total revenue is another challenge for the company.

Figure: Apple’s stock performance compared to the S&P 500. Source: YCharts

Despite the bull run, Apple’s stock performance in YTD has been lagging behind S&P 500. Most of the stock price growth has been due to multiple expansion in the valuation. This increases the risk of a correction if key metrics come below the expectations.

Apple’s Vision Pro dilemma

Earlier this year, Apple had hyped the launch of its Vision Pro. Back in March 2024, I forecasted that the device would see modest demand. This has proven right as Apple reduced the orders for this device. Apple will find it difficult to compete against Meta’s product lineup in this segment. In the recent Meta Connect event, Meta reduced the price of its Quest 3S to $299. It is difficult to see how customers would be willing to pay 10X the price for Apple’s Vision Pro compared to Meta’s alternatives.

Meta has reported annual losses of close to $15 billion for the last three years. Apple would need to at least make a similar investment in order to build its VR segment. Apple’s management has been trying to protect its margins, and it is highly unlikely that the company would be able to absorb similar losses in order to build its VR business.

Figure: Meta’s losses in Reality Labs for the last few quarters. Source: CNBC, Meta filings

Even a cheaper Vision Pro might not be able to compete against Meta. We have already seen the poor performance of Apple’s smart speaker segment which was trying to compete against Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG).

Pricey Valuation

Even the most bullish analysts for Apple will accept that Apple’s valuation is quite high compared to its historical average. The PE ratio of the last 10-year median for Apple stock is 20 while it is 28 for the last 3 and 5 years. On the other hand, the current PE ratio is close to 36. The forward EPS growth projection is nowhere close to justifying this elevated valuation level.

Figure: Historical PE and PS ratio of Apple. Source: YCharts

Figure: EPS estimate of Apple for next two years. Source: Seeking Alpha

A simple comparison of forward EPS estimate of Apple and Alphabet shows how high Apple’s valuation has become. Alphabet’s EPS for 2 fiscal years ahead is $10 which makes the forward PE ratio equal to 16.5. Hence, Apple’s forward PE ratio of 28.1 for fiscal 2026 is 75% higher than Alphabet’s.

Figure: Comparison of price and EPS estimate of Apple and Alphabet. Source: YCharts

Within the big tech group, Apple has one of the highest forward PE ratio. Even Meta, despite the recent bull run, has a forward PE ratio of 20 for the fiscal year ending 2026.

Apple’s revenue growth projection is lower than all other big tech players. Apple is also facing headwinds due to new fines and regulations. EU has recently announced a $17 billion fine on Apple, which can take up a big chunk of its EPS if it is finalized. Apple’s juicy commissions from App Store and licensing rights are also under threat from different regulators. At the same time, Apple’s China revenue base continues to be under threat due to geopolitical tensions and strong local competition.

Investors looking for a long-term buy-and-hold play in Apple stock need to consider these challenges. Any decline in iPhone sales or poor performance in Services can cause a correction in Apple stock from the current elevated levels.

Investor Takeaway

Apple has hyped up its recent iPhone iteration due to new AI services. However, external data shows that the demand has been sluggish. If Apple reports another quarter of YoY iPhone sales decline, we could see a big correction in the stock. Apple’s Vision Pro is also facing intense competition from Meta, which has invested over $15 billion annually to build the VR segment. A modest performance of Vision Pro can be another red flag for the company.

Apple’s forward PE ratio of fiscal year ending 2026 is 28 compared to only 16.5 for Alphabet. According to this metric, Apple is 75% more expensive than Alphabet while showing a significantly lower revenue growth trajectory. Apple’s current valuation is also elevated when we look at recent historical average for the stock. This increases the risk for investors and lowers the margin of safety. I believe there are few upsides to the stock at the current price while the company continues to show big risks due to fines, regulations, and higher competitive pressure.

data.title: Reassessing ASML Holding: Why I Am Lowering The Stock To A Hold Rating
data.description:
When I last wrote about ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) on June 13, 2024, news had just hit the market that the company would ship its latest lithography systems for manufacturing chips to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) in 2024. At the time, it seemed like the semiconductor cycle would rapidly rebound from its 2023 lows. That assessment turned out to be wrong. ASML's third quarter 2024 earnings call on October 16 made it clear to investors that the new semiconductor upcycle was less robust than initially thought. The stock was down 4.70% for the year on October 21. It is also down 31.42% from my June buy recommendation, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 7.24% rise.

The company's third quarter 2024 earnings webcast was mainly bad news for investors. Management reduced the 2025 revenue guidance due to a slowdown in demand for semiconductor manufacturing equipment. Some of ASML's most prominent customers are not seeing as much end demand for personal computers ("PCs") and smartphones as they initially expected, and they have lowered orders for new equipment. Additionally, orders from China have started to dry up. Management faces a lot of near-term uncertainty over the health of the semiconductor chip market. Still, ASML bulls can look to Artificial Intelligence ("AI") to drive meaningful revenue growth in the long term.

This article will discuss the market conditions ASML faces and how they impact the company's fundamentals. It will also examine a few risks, the company's valuation, and why I am lowering the stock to a hold.

Market conditions

One of the company's growth drivers is the global expansion of chip fabrication plants. The European Chips Act, the Chips for America Act, and the FABS Act were supposed to create more demand for ASML's lithography systems since each new plant needs them. However, some of ASML's most prominent customers have recently delayed operationalizing some of these planned new plants. For instance, in September, Intel (INTC) announced it was delaying the construction of fabs in Poland and Germany for two years. An Intel press release said, "We will pause our projects in Poland and Germany by approximately two years based on anticipated market demand."

On October 18, 2024, Samsung paused the shipment of ASML's lithography tools to its proposed Texas plant. A Seeking Alpha article stated (emphasis added), "Samsung Electronics has postponed taking deliveries of ASML's chipmaking machine for its upcoming facility in Texas as it has yet to get any major customers for the project, Reuters reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter."

ASML management emphasized on its earnings calls that the end demand for the traditional end markets that its customers rely on has been lackluster. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Christophe Fouquet said on the third quarter 2024 earnings call (emphasis added), "In logic, the slow recovery of end markets such as mobile and PC, together with specific competitive foundry dynamics, has resulted in a slower ramp of new nodes at certain customer who are as a result pushing out some of their fabs and changing their litho demand timing." Although CEO Fouquet didn't name the company, he likely refers to Intel's woes.

The memory market has also suffered from this slow-end demand, as smartphones, tablets, other mobile devices, and PCs all utilize DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory), ROM (Read-Only Memory), and NAND long-term memory, which are all memory types manufactured using ASML's equipment. When mobile device and PC manufacturers see lower demand for their products, they order fewer logic and memory chips from Intel, Samsung, and others. With less need to manufacture logic and memory chips, these integrated circuit manufacturers order less chipmaking equipment from ASML and other semiconductor equipment manufacturers, which shows up in fewer orders. ASML's third quarter 2024 net system bookings were EUR2.633 billion, declining sequentially from EUR5.567 billion in the second quarter. Some of the market's adverse reaction to the earnings report was the lower number of orders, which affected its future revenue-making potential.

ASML also forecasts its 2025 sales to Chinese customers to drop substantially from 47% in the third quarter. On the third quarter earnings call, Chief Financial Officer Roger Dassen said (emphasis added), "We...now expect our China sales to be around 20% of our total revenue next year, trending back towards our historical China percentage and in-line with its share of the backlog." ASML had built up a large backlog in China due to its inability to complete orders due to supply chain disruptions post-pandemic. The previous CEO, Peter Wennink, discussed these supply chain issues in the company's 2022 annual report:

The aftermath of COVID-19, the ongoing war in Ukraine and struggles among some of our supply chain partners to deliver according to our agreed plans due to material shortages have combined to cause significant turbulence and meant that we were unable to give our customers what they needed all of the time. Ultimately, we have seen the global chip shortage that first appeared in 2020 continue through 2022.

Over the last two years, ASML began rapidly fulfilling orders in its Chinese backlog. Now that it has cleared that backlog, it expects Chinese sales to return to historical norms. Additionally, debates around Chinese export controls have made management more cautious in its sales forecasts.

The company forecasts that its customer support business, which it calls its installed base business, will outperform in 2025. CFO Roger Dassen said on the earnings call:

We do believe that the installed base business, both on the service side and also on the upgrade side, is going to grow in a quite healthy way to -- in 2025. So yes, we do project that it will that there will be quite healthy growth in that. So there will be healthy double-digit growth as we currently see it in the installed base management in '25 in comparison to '24.

On the surface, the installed business outperforming during a period of low demand seems excellent. However, some might view customers spending more money upgrading and optimizing their existing equipment's performance instead of paying for a new machine as a bad sign. The company's bread and butter business sells customers new machines costing up to $380 million per machine.

All of the above are short-term concerns pressuring the stock price. However, the company believes the business has several secular trends with long-term revenue upside potential. ASML CEO Fouquet discussed several upside trends favoring the company on the third quarter earnings call:

As we look out longer-term, consistent with what we have previously stated, the secular growth drivers in the semiconductor end markets are still very much intact. AI, energy transition, electrification, among other applications, continue to provide a very strong and very positive perspective to our industry and ASML business. The expanding application space, along with increasing lithography needs on future technology nodes, drive demands for both advanced and mainstream nodes. In line with most of our industry peers, we continue to see AI as an upside and continue to watch carefully how this will affect us on the short and in the long-term. Despite some of the push outs we have discussed [Intel and Samsung], we continue to prepare for a number of new fabs that are being built across the globe to address the future demand and needs of the industry. Those fabs are spread geographically, our strategy for our customer and our schedule to take our system. We will therefore continue to build capacity in order to respond to the demand increase that we expect throughout the remainder of this decade.

The most apparent revenue growth driver is AI, which needs logic and memory chips that are powerful and fast enough to run very demanding generative AI applications in the cloud and enterprise data centers. More efficient semiconductors for edge computing and IoT AI applications are also needed. These chips require increasingly smaller chip designs, which the industry calls nodes. Today, the chip industry makes chips as small as 3nm (nanometer), and my recent article on Taiwan Semiconductor mentioned how it was preparing for the 2nm node. Manufacturers need ASML's most advanced technology, High NA EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) lithography machines, to most effectively manufacture chips at 3nm nodes and below. Less obvious growth drivers are renewable energy (solar and wind) that require advanced power electronics and control systems, energy storage systems like batteries, electric vehicles, an improved electrical grid, and other vital applications like 5G. Investors who believe the above applications will drive long-term demand for ASML's devices remain Bullish despite the stock's short-term setback.

Company fundamentals

ASML grew total revenue by 13.04% year-over-year to $8.21 billion. CFO Dassen said on the earnings call that total revenue was "above the high end of our guidance driven by more DEEP UV system sales as well as higher installed base management sales." The Deep UV (DUV) system sales likely came mainly from the company's fulfillment of backlog to China. Until now, the U.S. export controls have allowed sales to China because DUV lithography doesn't make chips below 7nm very effectively. The U.S. controls currently ban just EUV sales.

Customers upgrading and improving their machines instead of buying new ones were likely responsible for higher installed base management sales.

Total system sales, which are sales of its lithography machines, were $6.46 billion, consisting of $2.30 billion of EUV sales and $4.16 billion of non-EUV sales. Note that the charts below report in EUR instead of dollars. The exchange rate when the company reported was around $1.095 to EUR1.00. In the bar chart labeled Sales in lithography units, ArFi stands for Argon Fluoride Immersion (wet) and is the most advanced DUV technology. Argon Fluoride is the gas used in the laser. ArFdry is Argon Fluoride dry and is a less advanced DUV. KrF stands for Krypton Fluoride and is the initial DUV tool. I-line uses a mercury arc lamp. Each gas produces a different wavelength, with I-line producing the largest wavelength at around 365 nm and Argon Fluoride producing the smallest wavelength in the DUV spectrum at 193 nm. The smaller the wavelength, the smaller the node the laser can produce. The older lithography units still have use cases, so ASML still sells this older technology. Investors want to see sales of EUV (the latest technology) continue growing year-over-year.

ASML's third-quarter gross margins were 50.80%. Operating margin was 32.69%.

ASML's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash flow from operations ("CFO") to sales was 18.37%, a steep drop from early 2022. Likewise, CFO dropped to $5.23 billion, and FCF dropped to $3.07 billion. If you are wondering why there is a lot of bearish sentiment towards the stock, this drop in cash flow is one likely culprit.

The following commentary from CFO Dassen on the third quarter earnings call provides the reason for why TTM CFO-to-Sales, TTM CFO, and TTM free cash flow ("FCF") have dropped:

However, as mentioned last quarter, there continued to be pressure on free cash flow. This is primarily due to a relatively lower level of order intake and therefore less down payments as well as higher inventory level. The higher inventory level is primarily attributable to EUV, both High NA and Low NA driven by longer lead times in the build cycle as well as inventory in support of future ramp.

In essence, ASML must pay its suppliers well in advance of being able to build and deliver its lithography machines. The long lead times cause inventory to build up, leaving a substantial amount of the company's cash tied up in inventory. With fewer down payments, the company has more money going out to suppliers or tied up in inventory than cash coming in, pressuring cash flow.

If the company maintains its dividend of EUR1.52 per ordinary share, it will produce EUR6.08 per ordinary share in 2024, slightly behind last year's EUR6.10.

At the end of its third quarter, ASML had $5.567 billion in cash and short-term investments and $5.241 billion in long-term debt. Be aware that if you look at these numbers on other websites, they may be slightly off because websites may use differing exchange rates. It has a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.29, which means it finances its operations with equity more than debt, which is financially safer. The company's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 0.44, which means ASML can cover its debt with its operating profits. The company likely has the financial strength to weather tough times.

If the company meets its full-year 2024 revenue and gross margin guidance, revenue will grow only 1.4% over 2023, and gross margins will be down 70 basis points.

Additionally, CEO Fouquet stated in the earnings press release:

Based on the recent market dynamics as mentioned above, we expect our 2025 total net sales to grow to a range between €30 billion and €35 billion, which is the lower half of the range that we provided at our 2022 Investor Day. We expect a gross margin between 51% and 53%, which is below the range we then provided, mainly related to the delayed timing of EUV demand.

Management's previous guidance for 2025 was for revenue of EUR 30 billion-EUR 40 billion and for gross margins of 54%-56%. The lowered 2025 guidance was disappointing as investors were expecting a banner year. As a result of the weak outlook, Wall Street analysts lowered revenue and EPS estimates. Analysts also lowered price targets.

ASML will hold Investor Day 2024 on Thursday, November 14, 2024, which should inform investors more about the company's near-term expectations. Until then, the stock's uncertainty may be high enough that risk-averse investors may want to avoid it.

Valuation

ASML's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 9.876, below its three-, five-, and seven-year median. As a result, some may consider the stock fairly valued or slightly undervalued based on its P/S ratio. If it sold at its seven-year median, the stock price would be $735.50. If it sold at its five-year median, the stock price would be $820.62, around 14% above its October 22 closing price of $720.91.

Its one-year forward P/S ratio is 8.052. Based on where it traded over the past five years, the market likely overvalues the stock when it trades at a one-year forward P/S ratio above 12.0 and undervalues it below 8.0. As a result, the market may fairly value the stock at current prices.

ASML has a one-year forward price/earnings (P/E)-to-growth ("PEG") ratio of 1.00 (one-year forward P/E of 27.54 divided by analysts' estimated 2025 EPS growth of 27.46%).

Some investors allow a growth stock to have a PEG ratio of 2.0 before considering it overvalued. If it traded at a 2.0 one-year forward PEG ratio, the stock price would be $1,437.80, or 99.44% above its October 22 closing price. According to these calculations, the stock price could double before the market considers it overvalued. As with all valuation methods, market sentiment often determines where a stock will trade. Therefore, don't expect the stock to jump 100% immediately if the short-term outlook looks bleak. It could potentially take one to five years before sentiment surrounding ASML improves. Additionally, some valuation methods suggest that the market grossly overvalues the stock.

Shareholder yield describes how much money a company returns to investors through cash dividends, stock repurchases, and debt reduction. Generally, proponents of using shareholder yield to value a company believe that when a stock trades at the high end of its range, the market undervalues it. When trading at the low end of the range, the market overvalues it. The market may overvalue ASML using that rationale. Be aware that a potential reason this metric has dropped is that, with less FCF, the company cannot return cash to shareholders at previous rates. Eventually, the company should start producing cash flow again, and management will likely use the increased FCF to improve shareholder yield. So, take the current number with a grain of salt.

Seeking Alpha Quant grades its valuation as a D, which may be harsh. In my opinion, the market currently fairly values the stock at its October 22 price.

Risks

If Chinese export controls worsen, ASML may underperform its revenue growth forecasts for China of around 20%, further hurting investor sentiment. This risk is out of the company's control and depends on geopolitical politics concerning the U.S.-China relationship.

One significant risk unimaginable for some is that another company will develop an alternative to ASML's lithography system. That day may already be here. Canon (OTCPK:CAJPY)(OTCPK:CAJFF) hopes to overcome ASML's substantial lead with its new nanoimprint lithography machine, recently shipped to the Texas Institute for Electronics. If Canon's lithography tool proves it's competitive with ASML's offering, the narrative that ASML is a monopoly goes out the window. The company becomes subject to potentially losing market share and having its margins squeezed.

Additionally, alternative technologies like carbon nanotube field-effect transistors may eventually make silicon obsolete. People should avoid thinking that investing in ASML as a long-term investment (five years or more) is a slam dunk. The stock has long-term risks. Its substantial lead in EUV may not protect it from disruption by new technology over the long term.

Reducing ASML to a Hold

ASML has lumpy revenue, meaning its revenue and growth can vary wildly from quarter to quarter. Investors who are currently disappointed in the company's performance after looking at third-quarter results may be cheering wildly as the company potentially produces record quarterly results two to four quarters from now. The end demand for chips can potentially change everything. Suppose AI-based smartphones and PCs rejuvenate those products' growth rates; manufacturers that delayed chip fabrication plans may decide to restart ramping up those facilities to meet what they see as improving demand. Such an event could see these manufacturers placing more orders and taking deliveries of ASML tools. Consequently, the market would likely return to full-bull mode sentiment.

Alternatively, some believe the company's revenue growth potential could remain lackluster compared to its valuation, resulting in further stock declines. The short-term picture is cloudy enough that momentum investors and people inclined to look for near-term outperformance should avoid buying on the dip. Other stocks in the semiconductor space potentially have a better risk versus reward. I am reducing my rating to a Hold.

data.title: EWH: The Recent Surge May Be A Dead Cat's Bounce
data.description:
Introduction

Hong Kong’s stock market has been through a roller coaster ride in the past month. The Hang Seng Index has risen by as much as 30% in a few weeks, and reached its near-term high on October 7. However, it has since retreated by about 10% from its peak. Many investors are wondering if this is a reversal of the multi-year decline that has started back in 2017, or whether this is a dead cat’s bounce. In this article, we will analyze iShares MSCI Hong Kong ETF (NYSEARCA:EWH) and provide our analysis and insights.

Investment Thesis

EWH has a portfolio of about 33 large-cap stocks from Hong Kong. The fund tracks the MSCI Hong Kong Index. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.5% is not cheap, but understandable as it invests in foreign equities. The fund has an attractive 4.2%-yielding dividend. EWH’s portfolio has a high exposure to financials and real estate sectors. These sectors are being impacted by the burst of China’s housing bubble and the weakening Chinese economy. Although EWH’s valuation is not expensive, considering its risk and reward profile, we think investors may want to seek other alternatives instead.

Fund Analysis

Government stimulus was the reason behind the recent surge

Let us first take a look at EWH’s fund price performance year-to-date. As can be seen from the chart below, EWH has been in a rangebound for most of 2024. However, its fund price has risen from about $16 per share in mid-September to more than $20 in early October, before declining to about $18 per share. The reason for EWH’s strong surge was due to the large stimulus plan by the Chinese government.

A portfolio of large-cap value stocks that focuses on financial and real-estate sectors

EWH’s decline of about 10% after reaching its near-term peak was likely due to investors’ concern about whether this stimulus package is enough or not. As we know, the city’s prosperity in the past has tied to the prospering Chinese economy in the past 2~3 decades. Hong Kong has acted as an important financial centre for China and the region. This is the reason why Hong Kong's stock exchange has a lot of financial services stocks. Therefore, it is unsurprising to see that EWH has a high exposure to financial services stocks. As can be seen from the chart below, the financial services sector represents about 46.4% of EWH’s total portfolio. The fund also has an 18.8% exposure to the real estate sector. Together, these two sectors represent 65.2% of EWH's portfolio. That is nearly two-thirds of the portfolio.

The problem of having a high exposure to the financial services and real estate sectors is problematic. This is because the Chinese economy is quite fragile as the country's economy is in the process of a housing market bubble burst. The burst of the housing bubble will typically take at least a few years, as it simply takes time for housing companies to clear these massive housing inventories and repay their debts. Therefore, any quick fix such as a government stimulus package may stabilize the market in the short term, but will likely not be able to reverse the trend in the long run. Multiple stimulus packages over the next few years will likely be necessary. In this environment, EWH’s financial and real estate stocks will likely continue to face challenges in the near-term.

EWH’s long-term earnings growth rate has been declining

As we have just discussed, EWH’s exposure to China’s weakening economy is not good news for the fund. As can be seen from the chart below, the consensus long-term earnings growth rate for stocks in EWH’s portfolio has been on a declining trend since the peak of 20% reached in 2012 to only 3.4% today. Therefore, EWH’s long-term growth outlook is not bright at all.

Valuation not expensive but still not attractive

Finally, let us look at EWH’s valuation. EWH currently trades at a forward P/E ratio of 12.7x. The fund has typically trade between 13x and 18x in the past 30 years. Therefore, EWH’s valuation is not expensive at all. However, this lower valuation may be partially justified due to the weak earnings growth outlook that we have discussed. Hence, we do not think this valuation as cheap either.

Political risk should not be ignored

One primary risk investors should keep in mind is political risk. This is because China is not a democracy. The communist government has often changed its economic policies in the past without any warnings. This uncertainty can be very problematic for the economy and also for the market. In addition, the Hong Kong government’s restriction on freedom of speech and human rights is also not good for the economy, as many middle-class and wealthy citizens will likely want to immigrate out of the district to other countries. This will weaken the district’s economy in the long run.

Investor Takeaway

After considering EWH’s risk and reward profile, we do not think EWH is an attractive investment choice. Therefore, we think investors may want to continue to stay on the sidelines and seek other alternatives instead.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Range Resources: Positive Guidance Revisions With Its Q3 2024 Earnings
data.description:
Range Resources (NYSE:RRC) is a pure-play Appalachian natural gas producer that has managed to weather low natural gas prices in 2024 with the help of its hedges. It generated $294 million in free cash flow during the first three quarters of 2024 with the benefit of $363 million in derivative settlements during that period.

With NYMEX gas prices expected to be over $1 higher in 2025 compared to 2024, Range may be able to generate a bit over $500 million in free cash flow next year despite having fewer hedges.

Range's Q3 2024 report was good and resulted in improved guidance around both production and costs. Range has shown the ability to maintain (and marginally increase) production with one completion crew.

That being said, Range appears largely fairly priced at the moment as I estimate Range's value at approximately $32.50 per share based on long-term (after 2025) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Guidance Updates

Range made some positive guidance updates with its Q3 2024 report, boosting production expectations while reducing costs and highlighting stronger realized prices for its NGLs.

Range now expects its 2024 production to average approximately 2.17 Bcfe per day (including 30+% liquids). This is around 1% higher than its initial expectations for 2024 production, as well as approximately 1.5% higher than its 2023 production levels. Range attributes the improved production expectations to optimized gathering and compression, as well as positive well performance.

Range's 2024 capex is now expected to end up in the upper half of its original guidance. Range has been continually running one completion crew, which maintains production levels as well as adds a bit to its well-in-process inventory.

Range's operating costs have also trended slightly better than expectations, and it has reduced its expense guidance by a total of approximately $0.06 per Mcfe compared to its original guidance.

As well, Range has made some minor positive revisions to its differential expectations for natural gas, and it realized a couple of dollars per barrel more than expected for its NGLs compared to Mont Belvieu pricing.

Potential 2025 Outlook

I am assuming that Range will average 2.2 Bcfe per day in 2025 production, which is 1% higher than its current 2024 guidance.

At current 2025 strip prices (including $3.20 NYMEX gas), Range would be projected to generate $2.806 billion in revenues after hedges. Range is projected to have $141 million in positive hedge value in 2025. Range has significantly fewer hedges than in 2024, but its hedges are still helpful.

I have assumed a negative $0.40 differential for Range's natural gas in 2025, along with NGLs that realize a couple of dollars per barrel above Mont Belvieu. These differentials are similar to Range's 2024 differentials.

This is before dividends, which add up to $77 million per year, currently at a $0.08 per share quarterly dividend.

Range had $1.44 billion in net debt at the end of Q3, 2024, within its target range of $1.0 billion to $1.5 billion in net debt. It has made a good amount of progress reducing its debt over the past few years.

Range does have $618 million in outstanding 4.875% unsecured notes due May 2025 that it may redeem with a combination of cash on hand and its credit facility (that currently has no outstanding borrowings).

Notes On Valuation

Range may be able to generate approximately $725 million in unhedged free cash flow at my long-term (after 2025) commodity prices of $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas. This also assumes a realized price of $25 per barrel for NGLs and no cash income taxes. As well, this assumes a maintenance capex budget of approximately $600 million per year.

At a relatively favorable 9% free cash flow yield, this results in an estimated value of approximately $33.40 per share for Range. Adjusting for weaker natural gas prices in 2025 reduces Range's estimated value to approximately $32.50 per share.

Range's unsecured notes due 2030 are currently trading at a 5.9% yield-to-maturity. A 9% free cash flow yield (without cash income taxes) would translate into around a 7% free cash flow yield (as a full cash income taxpayer). Thus, I don't think it would be appropriate to value Range at a lower free cash flow yield than 9% (without cash income taxes).

At $32.50 per share, Range would also be valued at an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple of roughly 6.0x at those long-term commodity prices. I'd consider $32.50 per share a relatively generous valuation for a long-term $3.75 NYMEX gas environment.

Conclusion

Range's Q3 2024 earnings report was pretty good as it made some positive revisions to its production and cost guidance as well as its differential expectations for the full year.

Despite these positive revisions, Range is still hindered by weak natural gas prices, although its hedges should at least allow it to generate around $400 million in 2024 free cash flow.

For 2025, Range is currently projected to generate a bit over $500 million in free cash flow at current strip of around $3.20 NYMEX gas.

I believe that Range is close to fairly priced for a long-term (after 2025) $3.75 NYMEX gas environment currently, and estimate its value at $32.50 per share in that scenario.

data.title: XLV: Here Is A Good Risk-Return Ratio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV). In addition to being characterized by resilience, the sector is one of those with the best prospects for earnings growth in Q3 and Q4.

Although the valuation based on comparative multiple analysis is not the most attractive, the entire US market is expensive because company margins are at their highest. Additionally, the sector was one of those that demonstrated the best returns in the last interest rate cut cycles in the US.

Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF

The Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF seeks to replicate the performance of American healthcare companies in the biotechnology, pharmaceutical, healthcare, equipment and health insurance sectors.

The ETF has 62 invested holdings and has a management fee of 0.09%. By replicating the performance of the pharmaceutical sector, this ETF has the characteristics of showing resilience in times of crisis, given that the products in the sector generally cover basic needs. With the introduction made, let's move on to the thesis.

Positive Results Reviews

The healthcare sector is the third most important in the S&P 500, and its results were received with some pessimism by the market in 2022 and 2023. However, we can see below that the outlook has changed significantly.

With earnings growth prospects of 10% and 22% in Q3 and Q4, respectively, the healthcare sector's results are one of the most anticipated by the market, and this earnings momentum definitely corroborates my positive view of XLV.

The Valuation Is Not That Attractive, But...

As we can see, using the comparative evaluation method with the P/E multiple, the XLV ETF does not offer a safety margin for investment, as it is significantly above its own average over the last 3 years.

However, it is important to note that the US market as a whole is expensive, and this is for good reason. As we will see below, companies' margins are reaching their highest levels, justifying the more stretched valuations.

It is important for investors to be more diligent when choosing stocks and ETFs to invest in. In this sense, one piece of information caught my attention regarding the value of the healthcare sector, and we will see it below.

The Sector Stood Out In Recent Interest Rate Cut Cycles

The table below is part of a study of the last five interest rate cut cycles in the United States, comparing the performance of each sector and the S&P 500. Let's look at the data.

The healthcare and staples sectors are the ones that stood out the most in terms of average returns. Therefore, considering that the ETF has had a 3.5% drop in prices since September 18, when the Fed began cutting interest rates, there is a potential for an upside of 14% given the historical statistical returns.

In my view, it seems interesting to have exposure to a sector that is resilient in crises and has the potential for a 14% appreciation given the diversification, and this corroborates my recommendation to buy the XLV ETF.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

The first risk to the thesis is that it is not a consensus. As we can see below, Seeking Alpha's quant tools indicate a recommendation to hold the ETF, especially given its weak momentum.

Another relevant risk to the thesis is that four of the last five interest rate cuts have preceded a recession in America. This is why a resilient sector such as healthcare has stood out, but there is no indication that a recession is likely to occur at this time.

Finally, the upside potential was calculated based on statistical data. However, there is a big difference between the economy of the 80s and 90s and the current economy, so there is no guarantee that the returns will occur again.

The Bottom Line

The healthcare sector is characterized by its resilience in times of crisis, since most of its products are for basic needs. Additionally, there are strong expectations for the results of these companies.

The sector's valuation is stretched, as is the case for the entire American market. However, when we analyze historically, the healthcare sector has had excellent returns in interest rate cut cycles like the current one.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF. In my view, a 14% upside based on statistical data, together with the ETF's diversification and sector resilience, are very attractive.
